I like the goal you set. I also think it would be worthwhile to pick one issue at a time, and ideally also pick a specific problem that you would like to solve.
I like the goal you set. I also think it would be worthwhile to pick one issue at a time, and ideally also pick a specific problem that you would like to solve.

Exploration is always interesting, but without focusing on a specific problem, the risk is that it remains only interesting without becoming useful. Focusing on a problem instead helps you guide your design decisions.

text: Yeah need to correct the links to the demo so they don’t use SSL. Just change https:// to http:// in the URL and it’ll work.
Yeah need to correct the links to the demo so they don’t use SSL. Just change https:// to http:// in the URL and it’ll work.

But it depends on the version of SnS whether that error matters or not. There’s two branches of development at the moment. “Bidirectional” SnS, which is described in the SPLASH 2018 paper, doesn’t use the external solver so the error above doesn’t matter. The “v0.7.1" link is this “Bidirectional” SnS. “Bidirectional” SnS is better formalized and can operate on larger programs but can currently only perform “small” program changes (i.e. modification of literal strings, numbers, or lists). The other SnS branch, call it “Output-Directed” SnS, can perform larger program transformations and is what was demoed at the LIVE workshop (<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9JcKnJyufD8>). It’s available through the “icfp-2018-svg” link. Let me know if you have any questions.

attachments.title: LIVE 2018: SVG Programming By Direct Manipulation of Intermediates

As much as I dislike Slack, changing platforms is just going to be introducing a lot of friction. Yes, it’s annoying that moving isn’t easy, and it’s exactly what Slack is counting on as a business model, but let’s face it, no other tool is significantly better than Slack across the board. Sure, individual features are better in a lot of tools, but ultimately we’re just trading what some people like better with what other people like better. Is that worth trying to have ~200 people pack up all their things and move countries?

If the main worry is that we lose access to older posts: hands up, who has searched this Slack recently and found what you were looking for in a post from more than a month ago?

I think our time would be better spent thinking about how we can preserve some of the valuable content in here outside. Should we have a website showcasing all the projects we’re working on? Should we have a wiki where we try to curate the main recurring themes, technologies, important papers and books, etc.?

If we want to preserve knowledge, we have to take it somewhere else anyway. But I doubt that destination should be another discussion forum.

text:
Yeah there are a few competing concerns that are worth picking apart. I think the “hands up if you searched” question is chicken and egg; the fact that slack is so transient and knowledge is distributed and hidden implicitly in conversations, means that search is not powerful in the first place. My dream would be a chat2wiki bot :) 

But yes every group is different; the group I mentioned earlier moved sooner rather than later partly in response to feeling slack’s aggression towards open source / open standards.

I'm a Javascript React frontend dev. Right now I'm trying out ideas on the side in a few areas:
• Coloring interfaces. I'm actively working on an experiment for this. This work might be helpful for future app UI design tools. I'll post links on this soon.
• Responsive components. I want to set breakpoints at the component level, not at the page level.
• Random experiments in hierarchical and composable systems.

The hypothesis is that better primitives can unlock a lot of new possibilities, specifically for programming UIs.

Thanks <@U5TCAFTD3> for tweeting a link to this. It's very exciting that there's a community of people who are interested in the future of coding.

Slack’s notification policy is intrusive. The only option to constant notifications is to manually poll which is also burdensome. Slack encourages chatter, not long-term discussions. I am trying to limit these sources of interruptions to my work. Places like google groups offer  more civilized email digest options.
